source.reference: SciBench dataset, source: Engel & Reid 'Thermodynamics, Statistical Thermodynamics, & Kinetics', problem 8.25. Verified by Clausius-Clapeyron: ln(253/760) = (40656/8.314)(1/373.12 - 1/T2); -1.1003 = 4889.3 * (1/373.12 - 1/T2); 1/T2 = 0.002680 + 0.000225 = 0.002905; T2 = 344.2 K.

problem.statement: For water, ΔH_vaporization is 40.656 kJ/mol, and the normal boiling point is 373.12 K. Calculate the boiling point for water on the top of Mt. Everest (elevation 8848 m), where the barometric pressure is 253 Torr.
problem.task: Calculate the boiling point of water at 253 Torr and express in K.

normal_boiling_point.description: Normal boiling point of water at 760 Torr

pressure_everest.description: Barometric pressure at the top of Mt. Everest (8848 m elevation)

answer.description: Boiling point of water at Mt. Everest summit pressure

answer._comment: Clausius-Clapeyron approximation over a wide temperature range; ±1 K accounts for dH_vap variation

must_include: Apply the Clausius-Clapeyron equation to find the boiling point at reduced pressure
Use the normal boiling point and 760 Torr as the reference state
Recognize that lower pressure leads to a lower boiling point
must_not_include: Assume boiling point is linearly proportional to pressure
Use the ideal gas law directly to compute a boiling temperature

key_assumptions: ΔH_vap is approximately constant over the temperature range 344-373 K
Vapor phase behaves as an ideal gas
Molar volume of liquid is negligible compared to vapor
common_mistakes: Getting the sign wrong — boiling point should decrease at lower pressure
Mixing up which temperature/pressure is T1/P1 vs T2/P2
Using ΔH_vap in kJ but R in J without converting
Forgetting that 760 Torr = 1 atm is the reference pressure for normal boiling point

reasoning_checkpoints.checkpoint: Identifies this as a Clausius-Clapeyron problem: boiling point at non-standard pressure
Sets up the equation with correct reference state: T1 = 373.12 K, P1 = 760 Torr
Correctly rearranges to solve for T2: 1/T2 = 1/T1 - (R/ΔH_vap) ln(P2/P1)
Ensures unit consistency between ΔH_vap and R
Confirms result makes physical sense: lower pressure gives lower boiling point

boiling_point_depression._comment: Lower pressure must give lower boiling point

boiling_point_above_freezing._comment: Water should still be liquid at this reduced boiling point
